content.problemStatement:
In Chefland, there are NNN cities (numbered 111 through NNN) connected by N‚àí1N-1N‚àí1 bidirectional roads. Each city has exactly one garden. For each valid iii, let's denote the population of the iii-th city by AiA_iAi‚Äã and the initial number of fruits in its garden by BiB_iBi‚Äã; also, at any time, let's denote the current number of fruits in its garden by FiF_iFi‚Äã (Fi=BiF_i = B_iFi‚Äã=Bi‚Äã initially).

You are given a permutation PPP of the integers 111 through NNN. For each iii (1‚â§i‚â§N1 \le i \le N1‚â§i‚â§N), during the iii-th of the next NNN days, the people of the city PiP_iPi‚Äã do the following:


For each city jjj which is reachable from the city PiP_iPi‚Äã (including the city PiP_iPi‚Äã itself), visit the city jjj and consume min(APi,Fj)\mathrm{min}(A_{P_i}, F_j)min(APi‚Äã‚Äã,Fj‚Äã) fruits from the garden of the city jjj; afterwards, FjF_jFj‚Äã decreases by min(APi,Fj)\mathrm{min}(A_{P_i}, F_j)min(APi‚Äã‚Äã,Fj‚Äã).
Return to the city PiP_iPi‚Äã and cut off all the roads that are connecting it to other cities, so that nobody can visit the city PiP_iPi‚Äã or visit any other city from their city if it requires passing through the city PiP_iPi‚Äã.
For each valid iii, let DiD_iDi‚Äã be the first day when the number of fruits in the iii-th city's garden becomes zero, or ‚àí1-1‚àí1 if the fruits in its garden never end up all eaten. Find D1,D2,‚Ä¶,DND_1, D_2, \ldots, D_ND1‚Äã,D2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,DN‚Äã.
constraints: 1‚â§Pi‚â§N1 \le P_i \le N1‚â§Pi‚Äã‚â§N for each valid iii
1‚â§Ai,Bi‚â§1091 \le A_i, B_i \le 10^91‚â§Ai‚Äã,Bi‚Äã‚â§109 for each valid iii
the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 5‚ãÖ1055 \cdot 10^55‚ãÖ105
the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 5‚ãÖ1035 \cdot 10^35‚ãÖ103
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
Each of the next N‚àí1N-1N‚àí1 lines contains two space-separated integers uuu and vvv denoting that vertices uuu and vvv are connected by an edge.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,‚Ä¶,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1‚Äã,P2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,PN‚Äã.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã.
The last line contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,‚Ä¶,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1‚Äã,B2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,BN‚Äã.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing NNN space-separated integers D1,D2,‚Ä¶,DND_1, D_2, \ldots, D_ND1‚Äã,D2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,DN‚Äã.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The process is as follows:

On day 111, people from city 111 eat 111 fruit from each garden. Afterwards, there are [0,2,5][0, 2, 5][0,2,5] fruits in the gardens of cities [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3] respectively, so the answer for city 111 is 111, since the number of fruits in this garden becomes zero on day 111.
Then, on day 222, people from city 222 eat 222 fruits from each of the gardens in cities 222 and 333. Afterwards, there are 000 fruits in the garden of city 222 (so the answer for city 222 is 222, since the number of fruits in this garden becomes zero on this day) and 333 fruits in the garden of city 333.
Finally, on day 333, people from city 333 eat 333 fruits from the garden of city 333, so the answer for city 333 is 333.
